body:
`util.NonPointer(util.NativeType(context.Type))` handles this, but yes we should have tests

Given:
```
type Foo string
type Bar *Foo
type Qux Bar
type Zrb *Qux
```

NativeType() of Zrb == **string and NonPointer(**string) == string.

Good point on Atoi -- we should probably fix all such callsites to use ParseInt for base 10 explicitly.

ParseBool I would also be fine, we could just add our own util.Parse{Bool,Int} funcs.

I am not sure I buy this -- consider:

```
    // +k8s:optional
    // +k8s:minLength=0
    Field *string
```

That is  unambiguous and semantically different from `Field string`
Interesting - we use Unsorted elsewhere and it has not been a problem, but it does seem wrong!  We should fix them all.
I think NAK, see argument above

At some point we (I, probably) said "bytes is close enough" because every callsite I looked at used `len()`.  I did not consider openapi.  One other consideration -- max-length protection of 1000 runes could have max-serialized data of anywhere from 1000 to 4000 bytes.  Is the intention to limit the impact of too-long strings on the system?  Then bytes seems correct.  But it IS weird and ASCII-centric.   (parsing a string to runes is a bit more computationally expensive, too)

I would be OK to change them both to runes, but they should be the same

We have not added distinct error types for every common case but just very common ones (though immutable springs to mind as common).  We never add "too short" because it just was not common -- most fields have minLength of 1, making the "is required" error more useful.

I don't MIND adding this, but I don't want to have 100 of these.
Including it in the error is OK for too-short but risky for too-long (so much so that we don't do it).  We could convert it to a snippet for too-long (e.g. the first 10 + "..." + last 6 or something)

> I think we should try to only have one semantically correct way to represent something, and like Jordan pointed out, setting +k8s:minLength=0 is the same as just not validating for a minimum length so it doesn't do anything but add unnecessary generated validation logic.

I don't feel strongly, but I will argue anyway -- it expresses an explicit intention, as opposed to incidental behavior.

If I see a field with no minLength, does that mean it is supposed to allows 0, forgot to check, or is covered by something else (e.g. `+k8s:format`).  Even still, not every format is clearly checking minimums.

Should we demand that every string has a format tag and that every format document the minimum?  If so, we don't really need minLength or maxLength, those will just cause 2 errors for the same problem.

Should we demasnd that every string has minLength and maxLength?  Formats might double up on this and cause 2 errors for the same problem.

I think what I am saying is that we should let the API authors/reviewers decide whether and when to use which tags, and that minLength=0 *is* semantically meaningful.  More than that, disallowing it "because it doesn't mean anything" is worse than just having it mean nothing (like literally - we could choose to NOT emit a validation when the tag value is exaclty 0).
It SHOULD handle validations attached to typedefs.  If it does not, that's a bug
Can we please file a bug to do the same for Bool?
Can we please file a bug to fix all the other places that use Unsorted?

Can we make the docs for min and max be as close as possible?

```
       Description:    "Indicates that a string field has a {min,max}imum length.",    
        Payloads: []TagPayloadDoc{{    
            Description: "<non-negative integer>",    
            Docs:        "This field must be no more than X characters long.",    
        }},    
```

The X is correct - the docs are explaining what the tag means when this payload is used.
"Canonically" is not adding a lot of value, IMO, but whatever :)
...so it does work? (wrt other comment saying it didn't work)

I'd say we add maxBytes IFF we have a use-case for it.  Most formats (not all) check length internally anyway, so MOST OF THE TIME, we don't want a field to say both format AND maxLength (or you get 2 errors for the same problem).

Can we please file a bug to either:

a) make TooLong() take a generic ~string

or

b) change this to take a string and make the generated code cast to string (to avoid generic code bloat); and then do the same on TooLong

Ahh, right.  Yeah it does not work on the thing that Go calls an "alias" because Go's tooling doesn't give us enough information to do that.  That's intentional on their part - it is an alias, not a new type.  It should be behaviorally 100% identical in all regards.  `type Foo = Bar`

The thing that Gengo calls an "Alias" is what C would call a "typedef" and Go doesn't really have a distinct name for: `type Foo Bar`.  That should work in DV.
It would be nice to hard-fail if we find tags on an alias in Go, but I am not sure we can even do that without going down into the AST itself.
awkward: "if we assume all characters in the input are runes" -- they ARE all runes, but they are not all 4-byte runes.
Probably should be "character" since this is user-facing?
So we should drop this rule, then, yeah?
I think "chars" is jargon, can we agree that user-facing errors should be whole words?  We can fix openapi :)

RE test fail:  You marked all these fields as optional, which (for value-typed fields) means "not the zero value".  If we find the zero value we skip subsequent validation -- how can something be both "not specified" and "invalid value" at the same time?  :)

But if you mark them as required then you will get a required error.

So I think you have to make these "Min2" and set a non-empty, 1 char string.  Testing the zero-value of a non-pointer field is clunky because of the implied meaning of optional/required.

This is different than openapi native meaning, I know.
how I can tell it wasn't written by AI:   s/no more than/at least/
sort before maxLength?
same?
